item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was enjoying this scip a great deal as I read it. I was coming up with a variety of theories and ideas about why the boxes stole voices and what they did with them. I was thinking about whether there was a memetic component to their singing to draw more victims in. Then they ate the voiceless. That was a bit of a let down honestly. I sort of like the front entrance as a larder idea mentioned but it just seems very weak. Going to -1 mainly out of a sense of disappointment.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I'm good with this edit! Thanks, Wikiwalk. :3

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This article contains a reference to Dr. Kiryu, who is an established character used in many other articles on site. As such, a crosslink can be made between his name and the Kiryu Labs Hub.

Editing this:

Note: The last robot probe sent into SCP-1355 managed about two minutes before its cameras started malfunctioning. What's left of the subjects will have to stay there. —Dr. Kiryu

To this:

Note: The last robot probe sent into SCP-1355 managed about two minutes before its cameras started malfunctioning. What's left of the subjects will have to stay there. —Dr. Kiryu

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It needs some tests about the foundation sending deaf or mute people in. There are so many possibilities that are just left out without even a thought! But the rest of the scip is ok, so I'll upvote it.

item.title: Hi Image Team!
item.content:encoded: Just making a note here that both the images here were created by me, since they're pictures I took with my cell phone camera at a place I worked at one summer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The questionnaire was OK, except in any situation where you are trying to gather information you would never ask leading questions such as "Did it sound nice?". To me this was so out of character for an investigator that it would preclude my up-voting even if the rest of the article was Foundation balanced. As for the whole of the article I have much of the same reservations that others have already voiced.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (See logs for further details; in lieu of interviews, a simple written questionnaire was provided to each subject.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just curious, if they lose the ability to speak, how are they being interviewed? I don't believe it says that sign language or any other method is being used.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For one, they're not formatted properly. But they're just the same thing copy-pasted three times with different answers

If you mean that they're not formatted as standard audio interviews, that's intentional as the logs are written questionnaires (seeing as the subjects who return cannot speak). I'll make some edits clarifying that, though.

They either need beefed up or removed.

I agree. At the moment though, I'm having trouble coming up with questions that would illuminate the nature of the boxes without making it seem that the Foundation knows more than they would about the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but a lure who has so obviously been debilitated isn't very good bait.

That's the main reason the "voiceless" weren't aware of the fact that their voices were taken. I was thinking of writing a Tale that tied into this article, where the boxes whisper "Sing with us. Sing with us. Forever. Forever…" before taking the voice of a D Class. My idea was that the music was so overwhelmingly beautiful and cleansing that those who heard it would seem to be eternally happy because of it, and it would raise the curiosity of others.

What if they aren't being let go on purpose? The front office could be the boxes' larder. They "eat" their favorite part, the voice, first; then set the rest aside for later.

That's actually a great idea!

Problem is, I really don't know how you could convey that in the article

Unfortunately, same here. I'll do some revisions. Thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
they ate the voices, and got a new box or two with each voice, as opposed to them eating people entirely. Unless I missed something?

I was thinking of something along those lines—"juvenile" boxes would be the ones that possess a voice but have not mastered the ability to sing; "adult" ones would have needed some form of sustenance (humans, mice, etc.) to mature and develop their voices. I'm not sure how the Foundation would find that out, though, without capturing a specimen.

Additionally, I was thinking that the "adult" boxes would possess the thermal signature similar to that of humans, while the "juveniles" would not, so the population could never be correctly determined.

The people are coming out, telling others to come listen to this awesomeness, pattern repeats. I'd try to expand on that a bit more personally.

I work on it. There's still issues that I didn't see before that need to be worked out. Thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, it's pretty "wtf".

That said, I don't think the interviews are terribly effective. For one, they're not formatted properly. But they're just the same thing copy-pasted three times with different answers, and the answers aren't really illuminative after the first interview. They either need beefed up or removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The predator/prey dynamic doesn't ring true here. I like Firewoven's theory that the voiceless are let go to lure more prey in, but a lure who has so obviously been debilitated isn't very good bait.

What if they aren't being let go on purpose? The front office could be the boxes' larder. They "eat" their favorite part, the voice, first; then set the rest aside for later. The music makes sure that the prey sticks around and/or comes back later, if The Foundation wasn't nabbing them as soon as they showed up that is. Problem is, I really don't know how you could convey that in the article with out ruining the good that's already there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think a more interesting twist could be that they ate the voices, and got a new box or two with each voice, as opposed to them eating people entirely. Unless I missed something?

Actually the people WANTING to go back seems a bit interesting. Perhaps there's more to this than I thought. The people are coming out, telling others to come listen to this awesomeness, pattern repeats. I'd try to expand on that a bit more personally.

item.title: Re: sigh...
item.content:encoded:
The bacon would be, as I have found, something that produces an unexpected and exponentially creepier response from the object/subject.

I'm not sure what sort of "unexpected and exponentially creepier response" you'd be looking for. Do you think that the boxes should just attacking voiceless people shark feeding frenzy style?

Oh, and while you're at it, replace "robot drone" with something a little more professional. I don't care what.

Um… wasn't it "probe"? I'll see what I can find.

item.title: Re: sigh...
item.content:encoded:
The bacon would be, as I have found, something that produces an unexpected and exponentially creepier response from the object/subject.
In this case, I think more test subjects are necessary. For example:
1. A recently-deafened person (surgically for extra sick&twisted points)
2. Someone with congenital hearing imparement (born deaf, never heard a thing in their life)
3. Someone with no vocal cords (again, surgical for the win)

Oh, and while you're at it, replace "robot drone" with something a little more professional. I don't care what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But then if these victims come BACK they eat them? Why wouldn't they do it the first time? Also I have to agree that the finale is really underwhelming.

Should I add an instance in which a subject was left in the building for several hours after encountering the boxes and falling asleep and the boxes ate them? When I originally wrote the article, I was thinking that the boxes would see those with voices as "useful" and would deposit them at the entrance as a way of asking for more.

Should I add in a recording mentioning voices singing "more, more, more please"?

Also I have to agree that the finale is really underwhelming.

Any advice on how to fix it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would say my biggest issue with this is that the combined anomalous effects don't make sense. The boxes sing to them, I'm assuming as a lure, put them to sleep, remove their ability to vocalize, and drop them off at the entrance.

But then if these victims come BACK they eat them? Why wouldn't they do it the first time? Also I have to agree that the finale is really underwhelming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
or something about the nature of the voice takers themselves…

I originally thought of the voice takers as non-sentient beings, but you have a point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well to me, what gets me going is altered mental states, which was why I was so positive to the previous build up and let down by the 'food' aspect. I could imagine that if there is a very subtle implication of something further than merely food, or something about the nature of the voice takers themselves… I'm sorry, I don't have any solid ideas at the moment…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but to have such an interesting build up only to end up 'food' is a little down to me personally

Any suggestions on what sort of end wouldn't be a letdown? Also, other people might have the same opinion, so I'll definitely implement changes if there's enough approval of them.

Thanks again for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The best part, in my opinion? Using a questionnaire instead of doing an interview log. I don't know if that has been used in other articles, but here it fits perfectly in the entire theme.

Thanks!

I wasn't sure about the questionnaire at first because I hadn't seen it used in other articles; I'm glad you think it fits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand that you are reflecting 'voiceless' people as 'food'… but to have such an interesting build up only to end up 'food' is a little down to me personally. You don't have to change the SCP personally for me of course, that's just my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm…I like this one, especially since I've seen it with a lot of the problems fixed.

The best part, in my opinion? Using a questionnaire instead of doing an interview log. I don't know if that has been used in other articles, but here it fits perfectly in the entire theme.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is numbering inconsistency here. What I mean is that, the way you are writing numeric amounts is inconsistent, such as writing one numeric amount as 'twelve(12)' and the other one 'one'.

Will fix.

The tone here is a bit iffy, since 'a chorus of ethereal voices' is not exactly something you'd read in a scientific article like this. Perhaps you can better state it as 'report hearing multiple voices often described as 'ethereal' and a 'chorus'' Or something like that? I'm not honestly sure myself.

I'll take a look at that and reword it.

I don't personally agree with this, because I'm pretty sure the foundation is far more mature than 'oh no bad words'. If you insist, at least put it like [EXPLICATIVE REMOVED].

However, at the end, all it does is… kill them? I don't really understand at all… I thought it was 'building up' very well but the payoff was disappointing. No vote.

Noted. Will fix.

I was thinking that the boxes perceive the "voiceless" as food. I was thinking of adding a note that there were no mice or other living creatures found in the SCP.

item.title: Re: sigh...
item.content:encoded:
Don't fucking censor yourself. Also, excessive use of [expletive]s by the third guy seems EXTREMELY forced. It's an unspoken cliche of "that one test subject who swears a lot while the others are perfect little angels".

I was trying for three different responses, to show contrast in background and demeanor not affecting the subjects' susceptibility to the music they heard. Should I remove one of the interview logs to better convey that?

but there's zero suspense of disbelief in that thermal imaging log

Do you think the experiment log would work better if it was used to build suspense? Personally, I was using it in lieu of more text describing what the boxes did.

(I recommend bacon, pan-fried and chopped up right before serving so it doesn't lose its crunch…and a lot of salt and pepper).

Sounds great, but I'm not sure what "bacon" would be in this context. A better experimental log?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All lights within SCP-1355 are to remain on for twelve (12) hours each day
SCP-1355 is a one-story building

There is numbering inconsistency here. What I mean is that, the way you are writing numeric amounts is inconsistent, such as writing one numeric amount as 'twelve(12)' and the other one 'one'.

SCP-1355-1 report hearing a chorus of ethereal voices

The tone here is a bit iffy, since 'a chorus of ethereal voices' is not exactly something you'd read in a scientific article like this. Perhaps you can better state it as 'report hearing multiple voices often described as 'ethereal' and a 'chorus'' Or something like that? I'm not honestly sure myself.

[expletive]

I don't personally agree with this, because I'm pretty sure the foundation is far more mature than 'oh no bad words'. If you insist, at least put it like [EXPLICATIVE REMOVED].

However, at the end, all it does is… kill them? I don't really understand at all… I thought it was 'building up' very well but the payoff was disappointing. No vote.

item.title: sigh...
item.content:encoded:
Don't fucking censor yourself. Also, excessive use of [expletive]s by the third guy seems EXTREMELY forced. It's an unspoken cliche of "that one test subject who swears a lot while the others are perfect little angels".

Now for the actual content:
All I can say is "what?". I can tell you're trying to make something that's creepy, but there's zero suspense of disbelief in that thermal imaging log. It's just "subject went in, surrounded by boxes, disappears, boxes increase by X".

Don't mean to be a dick, but this article is like potato soup. Anyone can make it, but it's flavorless white paste until you get creative with the seasonings and extras (I recommend bacon, pan-fried and chopped up right before serving so it doesn't lose its crunch…and a lot of salt and pepper).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Try not to redact anything in the containment protocols.

Whoops. Will fix.

Also, is it necessary to kill everyone who even accidentally approaches the SCP? Because that's an awful lot of bodies to explain.

I was assuming that the perimeter would keep civilians out—I'll clarify. Thanks for pointing it out; I'll tone it down.

Otherwise, I kind of like the idea, and the execution is alright. Giving you my upvote.

Much appreciated. Thanks again for the feedback!

Edit: silly formatting errors… embarrassing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Try not to redact anything in the containment protocols.
Also, is it necessary to kill everyone who even accidentally approaches the SCP?
Because that's an awful lot of bodies to explain.

Otherwise, I kind of like the idea, and the execution is alright.
Giving you my upvote.
